Deployment & Post-Cutover.variance_note: Significantly underestimated — post-cutover support was 3x the estimate

insights.text: All three core technical phases matched their estimates exactly — A&D (475h), Implementation (424h), and Testing (475.5h) each came in at 100% of estimate. The CygNet work-type scoping methodology was highly accurate.

D&R came in 15h under estimate (207.5h vs 222.5h). Cleanup/Pre-work saved 8h (tag and device cleanup was faster than scoped), and the discovery phase itself saved 7h due to efficient requirements gathering.

Deployment & Post-Cutover drove the entire project overrun. Cutover itself was on-target (66.5h), but 184h of post-cutover support was completely unscoped — 2.77× the deployment estimate alone. Future projects must include post-cutover support as a standalone scope item.

Integrator hours dominated at 55% of technical time (1,008.5h) — consistent with standardization-type work where most hands-on CygNet configuration is Integrator-level. Expert (36%) handled analysis and testing leadership.

Testing consumed 475.5h — 26% of total technical hours (1,832.5h). The Architect-heavy testing phase (90h, 19% of testing) reflects significant server prep and validation work that is often underestimated in initial scopes.

Net project variance: +169.0h (+8.6%) over a 1,959.0h estimate — driven entirely by unscoped post-cutover support. Excluding that single line item, the project delivered within 15h of estimate across all other phases combined.
Total actuals (5,292h) ran 2.9× the original estimate (1,832h). This is the single most significant pattern — scope was dramatically underestimated at the outset.
Analysis & Design ran 612h actual vs 132h estimated — a 4.6× overrun. Design complexity (screens, facilities, point translation, custom scripts) was the primary driver.
Cleanup/Pre-work alone consumed 563.5h — 3× the entire estimated D&R phase. Tag cleanup, facility prep, and server setup were severely underscoped.

Implementation was Integrator-heavy (56%) and Specialist-heavy (25%) as expected for standardization work. Architect utilization dropped to 13% in implementation — consistent with the broader pattern.
Testing & UAT was the most accurately estimated phase: 348.5h actual vs 292h estimated (1.2× ratio). Testing scope was better-defined than earlier phases.
Deployment came in at 39.5h — well under the 80h estimate. Tight execution or scope reduction at cutover kept deployment lean.

CONOCO_DEL_STD.name: Conoco Phillips Delaware – Standardization + Merger
CONOCO_DEL_STD.scope_note: ~300K points, 84 pts/hr, 3 sites, 792 pre-built screens, 1 standardization + 1 merger in scope

totals.integrator_rate_note: Integrator billed at $105/hr on this contract (vs standard $135)

insights.text: This project carried two implementation phases in parallel: Standardization (690.5h) and a Merger (721.5h). Together they represent 69% of core project hours — nearly double a typical single-scope project.
Testing ran 940h (27% of total) driven by the dual-scope. Merger Testing alone was 617.5h and was Architect-heavy (25%) — indicating significant technical complexity in the merger validation layer.

Expert utilization was 0% across all implementation and testing phases — all technical execution was split between Architect, Integrator, and Specialist. This suggests work that required either senior architects or hands-on configuration specialists with no mid-level expert involvement.
Post-cutover support (128.5h) nearly equaled deployment (126h). For dual-scope projects, post-cutover support should be scoped as a separate phase — not assumed to be minor.
Invoiced revenue of $551,405.97 vs formula cost of $438,748 — representing a 25.7% premium above direct labor cost. PM overhead and project coordination account for this delta.

DGO was an Expert/Integrator-driven project — Architect utilization was only 2% of total hours (50h of 2,241h). This is the lowest Architect intensity in the dataset and suggests a well-defined scope executed by mid-level practitioners.

Implementation consumed 56.5% of total project hours (1,267h). Expert (611h) and Integrator (654.5h) split implementation nearly 50/50 — an unusually balanced split that indicates parallel workstreams.

Testing & UAT (525h) was 23.4% of total hours — consistent with the 21–28% historical range for reimplementation projects. The Expert-Integrator split in testing (281h/244h) mirrors the implementation pattern.

Discovery was lean at 57.5h (2.6% of project). For a standardization project with well-understood scope and existing CygNet infrastructure, this is appropriate — no Integrator discovery work was needed.
No Specialist hours were logged on this project — zero. DGO's scope did not include the specialist-intensive work (tag translation, UDC management) seen in larger Conoco engagements.

complexity_model._note: Calibrated from 10 completed projects. Use as starting point; adjust with scope notes.

Migration_to_Ignition._note: CygNet-to-Ignition or platform migration. Higher per-unit cost due to screen rebuild.

Reimplementation / Standardization._note: CygNet standardization across one or more regions

Upgrade._note: CygNet version upgrade. Scale primarily by number of servers/sites.
